title: The practice of being present without needing to fix anything
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
You don't have to fix anything. Really. 

 I used to think healing meant constant work. Constant fixing. Constant trying to make myself better. Every unco...
htmlContent:
You don't have to fix anything. Really.

I used to think healing meant constant work. Constant fixing. Constant trying to make myself better. Every uncomfortable feeling was a problem to solve. Every flashback was a failure. Every moment of disconnection meant I wasn't trying hard enough. Sound familiar? Here's the thing. That approach nearly broke me. It's not that effort is bad. It's that the need to fix everything is itself a trauma response. And it keeps us trapped in the very cycles we're trying to escape.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma gets stuck in the body. Not as a problem to be solved, but as an experience that needs to complete itself. He watched animals in the wild shake off life-threatening experiences and then just go back to grazing. No talk therapy. No journalling. No gratitude practice. Just the body knowing what to do when you get out of its way. That's what being present without fixing looks like. It's not passive. It's deeply active. But the action is one of allowing, not forcing.

I remember sitting with a client who was sobbing about how she couldn't "fix" her childhood. She had read every self-help book. Tried every modality. And still felt broken. I asked her a simple question. What if you just let yourself feel the sadness without trying to change it? She looked at me like I had suggested she stop breathing. That's how conditioned we are to fix. The nervous system interprets stillness as danger. Because for many of us, stillness was when the bad things happened. Stay with me here. Learning to be present without fixing is a radical act of renegotiating your relationship with your own body.

The fixer's trap and what it actually costs

There's a reason we become fixers. It's a survival strategy. If you could just figure out what was wrong with you, maybe you could finally be safe. Maybe you could finally be loved. Maybe the pain would stop. That's not your fault. That's a nervous system doing exactly what it evolved to do. But here's what happens over time. The fixing becomes the problem. You spend so much energy trying to change your experience that you never actually have it. You're always one step removed. Always watching yourself. Always judging. Always wondering if you're doing it right.

Bessel van der Kolk, in his work on trauma and the body, describes how the brain's alarm system gets stuck in overdrive. The amygdala keeps firing even when there's no actual threat. And what do we do? We try to fix the alarm. We read about it. We analyze it. We try to think our way out of a body-based response. It doesn't work. Because the alarm isn't a thought. It's a sensation. A vibration. A contraction. And the only way to let it settle is to be present with it without trying to silence it. Right? Think about it. When was the last time you just sat with an uncomfortable feeling without trying to make it go away?

I'll tell you what it cost me. Years. Years of trying to fix myself instead of being with myself. Years of reading about attachment theory while never actually feeling the loneliness in my chest. Years of practicing grounding techniques while never actually letting myself land. The fixer's trap is seductive because it gives you the illusion of control. But real healing doesn't come from control. It comes from surrender. Not giving up. Surrendering to what's actually happening in your body right now. One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). It doesn't promise a quick fix. It promises understanding. And sometimes that's the most healing thing of all.

What presence without fixing actually looks like

Let me be specific about this. Because I know the phrase "being present" can feel like wellness industry fluff. It's not. It's a specific practice that looks different from what you might think. Presence without fixing means you feel the tightness in your chest and you don't try to breathe it away. You notice the thought "I'm broken" and you don't argue with it. You let the tears come and you don't tell yourself to stop crying. You sit in the discomfort and you don't reach for your phone, your journal, your next coping strategy. You just stay. That's it. That's the practice.

And here's what makes it so hard. The nervous system will scream at you. It will throw up every distraction, every judgment, every memory of why this is a bad idea. Because from its perspective, staying present with pain is dangerous. It learned that lesson well. But here's the thing about the nervous system. It's not trying to hurt you. It's trying to protect you. And when you show it, over and over, that you can stay present without trying to fix, it slowly learns that the danger is over. That's not a metaphor. That's neurobiology. That's the work of Stephen Porges and his Polyvagal Theory. The body learns safety through repeated experience, not through intellectual understanding.

I practice this in small ways. When I notice my shoulders creeping up toward my ears, I don't immediately drop them. I just notice. I feel the tension. I let it be there. And something strange happens. After a few seconds, the tension starts to shift on its own. Not because I fixed it. Because I stopped fighting it. That's the paradox. The less you try to fix, the more things naturally settle. A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It doesn't tell you how to fix your parents or yourself. It just helps you understand. And understanding, without the pressure to fix, is medicine.

What to do when the urge to fix feels unbearable

I'm not going to pretend this is easy. Some days the urge to fix is so strong it feels like a physical compulsion. Your skin crawls. Your mind races. Your body wants to do something, anything, to make the discomfort stop. On those days, you don't need to be perfectly present. You just need to be a little bit present. Maybe you feel the floor under your feet for three seconds. Maybe you notice your breath for one inhale. Maybe you just say to yourself, "I'm having the urge to fix right now." That's it. That's enough. You're not failing. You're practicing.

Dan Siegel talks about the window of tolerance. It's the zone where you can be present with your experience without getting overwhelmed or shutting down. When you're outside that window, presence isn't possible. And that's okay. The practice isn't about forcing yourself to stay present when you can't. It's about noticing when you've left the window and gently bringing yourself back. Over and over. Without judgment. Without criticism. Without trying to fix the fact that you left. That's the practice. Notice. Return. Notice again. Return again.

And here's something I've learned the hard way. The urge to fix is often a sign that you're getting close to something important. The nervous system ramps up its defenses right before a breakthrough. So when you feel that desperate need to do something, to change something, to make it stop, pause. Just for a moment. Ask yourself, "What would happen if I didn't fix this?" The answer might scare you. But it might also set you free. A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). It helps you see the fixer part not as an enemy, but as a protector that needs to be thanked and reassured.

The grief of letting go of the fixer identity

This is the part nobody talks about. Letting go of the need to fix means letting go of a part of your identity. The fixer kept you going. The fixer gave you purpose. The fixer made you feel like you were doing something. Without that role, who are you? That's a real question. And it can bring up a lot of grief. I felt it. I felt empty when I stopped trying to fix myself. I didn't know what to do with my time. I didn't know how to relate to my body without a project. I didn't know how to just be.

That grief is part of the process. It's not a sign you're doing it wrong. It's a sign you're letting go of something that once kept you alive. And that deserves respect. Not fixing. Respect. You can sit with that grief. You can let it move through you. You can cry about it. You can feel the loss of the person you thought you needed to be. And then you can start to discover who you are underneath all that effort. A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link). It's not about fixing your shame. It's about making room for it.

I still catch myself trying to fix. I notice it most when I'm scared. When something feels uncertain. When I don't know what's coming next. My brain immediately starts looking for a solution. A plan. A way to make it better. And I've learned to say to myself, "I see you. I know you're trying to help. But right now, we're just going to sit here. We're going to let this be uncomfortable. And that's okay." It's not a one-time decision. It's a thousand small choices, every day, to stay present without fixing. Wild, right? That the thing we're so afraid of is actually the thing that heals us.

Sit with that. Just for a moment. Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Maybe nothing. Maybe a shift. Maybe a tightening. Whatever it is, it's okay. You don't have to fix it. You don't have to do anything with it. You can just let it be there. That's the practice. That's the return. Not to a better version of yourself. Not to a healed version of yourself. Just to yourself, as you are, right now.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
  • No Bad Parts (paid link)
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link)
